QCF1.tagline: Quantum and quantum-inspired methods for hard combinatorial optimization
QCF1.subtitle: quantum states, adiabatic and variational algorithms for combinatorial optimization
foundations: complex Hilbert spaces, unitary operators, and the postulates of quantum mechanics
qubits, tensor products, and multi-qubit state spaces
the adiabatic theorem and time-dependent Hamiltonian evolution
QUBO and Ising formulations of combinatorial problems
the variational principle and parameterized quantum circuits
statistical mechanics, energy landscapes, and Metropolis sampling

literature.title: A Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm
literature.authors: Edward Farhi, Jeffrey Goldstone, and Sam Gutmann

literature.title: Quantum Computation by Adiabatic Evolution
literature.authors: Edward Farhi, Jeffrey Goldstone, Sam Gutmann, and Michael Sipser

literature.title: Quantum annealing in the transverse Ising model
Ising formulations of many NP problems
A variational eigenvalue solver on a photonic quantum processor
literature.authors: Alberto Peruzzo, Jarrod McClean, Peter Shadbolt, Man-Hong Yung, Xiao-Qi Zhou, Peter J. Love, Alan Aspuru-Guzik, and Jeremy L. O'Brien

QCF1.build: Built an end-to-end quantum optimization pipeline in Python with Qiskit, PennyLane, and the D-Wave Ocean SDK that maps a real combinatorial problem to QUBO and Ising forms and benchmarks QAOA, quantum annealing, and quantum-inspired solvers head to head.
objectives: Explain the postulates of quantum mechanics using qubits, state vectors, and unitary operators
Derive single and multi-qubit gate algebra from linear algebra over complex Hilbert spaces
Formulate combinatorial problems as QUBO and Ising Hamiltonians
Analyze the adiabatic theorem and the theory underlying quantum annealing
Construct and parameterize QAOA circuits and reason about their cost and mixer Hamiltonians
Optimize variational parameters using classical optimizers in a hybrid loop
Implement quantum-inspired heuristics such as simulated and tensor-network annealing on classical hardware
Benchmark quantum, hybrid, and classical solvers on shared optimization instances
Evaluate noise, sampling overhead, and embedding limits on near-term devices
Build an end-to-end pipeline mapping a real problem to a quantum solver and back

weeks.title: Linear algebra and quantum states
weeks.lecture: Review of complex vector spaces, inner products, Dirac notation, and the qubit as a unit vector in a two-dimensional Hilbert space. Tensor products build multi-qubit state spaces.
weeks.practice: Set up the Python and Qiskit environment and represent qubit states and amplitudes numerically with NumPy.
weeks.project: Initialize the project repository and encode a small toy problem instance as a state vector.

links.title: Qubits and Quantum States, Quantum Circuits, Measurements - Part 1 (Qiskit)
Qubits and Quantum States, Quantum Circuits, Measurements - Part 3 (Qiskit)

weeks.title: Quantum gates, measurement, and circuits
weeks.lecture: Unitary operators, the Pauli and Hadamard gates, entanglement, and the Born rule for measurement probabilities. Circuit model semantics and reversibility.
weeks.practice: Build and simulate basic circuits in Qiskit, inspecting statevectors and measurement histograms.
weeks.project: Implement a circuit that prepares and measures the equal superposition over candidate solutions.

links.title: Qubits and Quantum States, Quantum Circuits, Measurements - Part 1 (Qiskit)

weeks.title: Combinatorial optimization and complexity
weeks.lecture: NP-hard problems, MaxCut, graph coloring, and the QUBO formulation. Reductions and the role of penalty terms in constrained problems.
weeks.practice: Translate sample problems into QUBO matrices and verify objective values by brute force on small instances.
weeks.project: Choose the project optimization problem and express it as a QUBO with documented constraints.

links.title: Quantum Computing Tutorial Part 1: Quantum annealing, QUBOs and more (D-Wave)
Introduction to the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm and Applications (Qiskit)

weeks.title: Ising models and energy landscapes
weeks.lecture: Mapping QUBO to the Ising Hamiltonian, spin variables, ground states, and the geometry of energy landscapes. Connection between minima and optimal solutions.
weeks.practice: Convert the project QUBO to an Ising Hamiltonian and visualize the energy landscape of a reduced instance.
weeks.project: Produce a verified Ising formulation of the project problem with a small validation instance.

links.title: Quantum Computing Tutorials Part 2: QUBOs and Embedding (D-Wave)

weeks.title: Adiabatic theorem and quantum annealing
weeks.lecture: The adiabatic theorem, time-dependent Hamiltonians, spectral gaps, and annealing schedules. Theory of how slow evolution tracks the ground state.
weeks.practice: Team presentation of the project specification: problem, Ising formulation, target solvers, and evaluation plan.
weeks.project: Deliver the written and oral project specification milestone.

links.title: Quantum Annealing (Quantum Computing at Davis)
Quantum Computing Tutorial Part 1: Quantum annealing, QUBOs and more (D-Wave)

weeks.title: D-Wave hardware and minor embedding
weeks.lecture: Physical annealers, qubit connectivity, chimera and pegasus topologies, minor embedding, and chain strength. Practical limits of current devices.
weeks.practice: Use the D-Wave Ocean SDK to embed and sample the project Ising model on a simulated or cloud sampler.
weeks.project: Obtain first annealing samples for the project problem and record solution quality.

links.title: Embedding on to the Quantum Processing Unit (D-Wave Webinar)
Quantum Programming Tutorial (D-Wave Qubits 2021)
Quantum Computing Tutorials Part 2: QUBOs and Embedding (D-Wave)

weeks.title: QAOA theory
weeks.lecture: The variational principle, cost and mixer Hamiltonians, the QAOA ansatz at depth p, and the p to infinity limit recovering adiabatic evolution. Performance bounds on MaxCut.
weeks.practice: Construct a depth one QAOA circuit for a small project instance and evaluate the expectation value.
weeks.project: Implement a working QAOA cost function for the project problem.

links.title: Introduction to the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm and Applications (Qiskit)
QAOA: A different perspective (PennyLane Tutorial)
Solving MaxCut with QAOA: Quantum Tutorial for 100+ Qubits (Qiskit)

weeks.title: Variational optimization and parameter training
weeks.lecture: Classical optimizers, gradient and gradient-free methods, parameter landscapes, barren plateaus, and warm starting. The hybrid quantum-classical loop.
weeks.practice: Team interim presentation: progress, annealing versus QAOA results so far, and obstacles.
weeks.project: Deliver the interim presentation milestone and a tuned QAOA parameter set.

links.title: Optimizing QAOA Circuits for Hardware (Qiskit Tutorial)

weeks.title: Simulated annealing and parallel tempering
weeks.lecture: Classical Metropolis sampling, temperature schedules, parallel tempering, and why these are strong baselines for quantum methods.
weeks.practice: Run simulated annealing and parallel tempering on the project instance and compare against quantum samples.
weeks.project: Add classical baselines to the project benchmark suite.

links.title: Quantum Computing Tutorial Part 1: Quantum annealing, QUBOs and more (D-Wave)

weeks.title: Tensor networks and digital annealers
weeks.lecture: Quantum-inspired tensor-network optimizers, mean-field methods, and commercial digital annealers. Where quantum inspiration helps without quantum hardware.
weeks.practice: Apply a tensor-network or specialized quantum-inspired solver to the project instance.
weeks.project: Integrate a quantum-inspired solver and log its results in the benchmark.

links.title: News on Tensor Networks for Machine Learning and Quantum Computing Simulation (Roman Orus, IPAM)

weeks.title: Noise, error, and mitigation
weeks.lecture: Decoherence, gate and readout error, sampling overhead, and error mitigation strategies for noisy intermediate-scale devices.
weeks.practice: Run QAOA under a noise model and apply a mitigation technique, measuring the effect on solution quality.
weeks.project: Quantify noise impact on the project QAOA results and apply mitigation.

links.title: Quantum Error Mitigation I (Zlatko Minev, BSS2023)
Quantum Error Mitigation 2 (Zlatko Minev, BSS2023)
Quantum Error Mitigation III (Zlatko Minev, BSS2023)

weeks.title: Benchmarking and scaling
weeks.lecture: Fair benchmarking methodology, time to solution, approximation ratios, and scaling behavior across solver families. Reporting pitfalls.
weeks.practice: Assemble a unified benchmark comparing all solvers on shared metrics and instance sizes.
weeks.project: Finalize the benchmark table and figures for the project.

links.title: Solving MaxCut with QAOA: Quantum Tutorial for 100+ Qubits (Qiskit)

weeks.title: Final project defense
weeks.lecture: Course synthesis: when quantum, hybrid, and quantum-inspired methods are justified, and the outlook for fault-tolerant optimization.
weeks.practice: Team final presentation with oral defense of methodology, results, and design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the final report, code, and benchmark with oral defense.

links.title: Scott Aaronson on the Possibility of Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing by 2028

project.summary: Teams take one real combinatorial optimization problem and carry it through the full quantum optimization pipeline. They formulate it as QUBO and Ising, then solve it with quantum annealing, QAOA, and quantum-inspired baselines, producing a rigorous head-to-head benchmark and a final defended report.

tools: Qiskit: build, simulate, and run gate-based circuits and QAOA
Qiskit Optimization: high-level QUBO and converter workflows
PennyLane: differentiable variational quantum optimization
D-Wave Ocean SDK: QUBO and Ising sampling on annealers
dimod: shared QUBO and Ising data structures and samplers
minorminer: minor embedding onto annealer topologies
NumPy: linear algebra and statevector manipulation
SciPy: classical optimizers for the variational loop
NetworkX: graph problem construction and visualization
Matplotlib: energy landscapes and benchmark plots
IBM Quantum Platform: cloud access to real quantum hardware
Jupyter: interactive notebooks for the running project

references.title: Quantum Computation and Quantum Information
references.authors: Michael A. Nielsen and Isaac L. Chuang
references.note: Standard reference for qubits, gates, and quantum theory

references.title: Quantum Computing: An Applied Approach
references.note: Hands-on coding with foundations and a math toolkit

references.title: A Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm
references.authors: Edward Farhi, Jeffrey Goldstone, and Sam Gutmann

references.title: Qiskit Documentation
references.note: Official Qiskit and Qiskit Optimization docs

references.title: PennyLane Documentation
references.note: Differentiable quantum programming and QAOA demos

references.title: Ising formulations of many NP problems
references.note: Catalog of QUBO and Ising encodings

QCF1.ai_usage: Students lean on AI coding assistants to vibe-code the quantum optimization pipeline: scaffolding Qiskit and PennyLane circuits, refactoring QUBO and Ising mappings, and wiring up the D-Wave Ocean SDK from natural language descriptions of the problem. They drive the assistant to call tools and MCP servers that query quantum simulators and cloud backends, generate small validation instances and unit tests for QUBO objective values, and synthesize parameter sweeps for QAOA and annealing schedules. AI is also used to read back, plot, and interpret benchmark results, sanity checking approximation ratios and flagging suspicious solver behavior before it reaches the report.

QCF2.tagline: AI, optimization, and risk for modern quantitative finance
QCF2.subtitle: portfolio theory, stochastic models, and machine learning for quantitative strategies
foundations: mean-variance portfolio theory and the efficient frontier
convex optimization, quadratic programs, and duality
Brownian motion, Ito's lemma, and stochastic differential equations
risk-neutral pricing and the Black-Scholes-Merton framework
coherent risk measures, value at risk, and expected shortfall
statistical learning theory and time-aware cross-validation
backtest overfitting and the multiple-testing problem

literature.title: The Pricing of Options and Corporate Liabilities
Building Diversified Portfolios that Outperform Out of Sample
Pseudo-Mathematics and Financial Charlatanism: The Effects of Backtest Overfitting on Out-of-Sample Performance
literature.authors: David H. Bailey, Jonathan M. Borwein, Marcos Lopez de Prado, and Qiji Jim Zhu

QCF2.build: Built a reproducible quantitative research pipeline in Python with cvxpy, PyPortfolioOpt, scikit-learn, and vectorbt that constructs risk-aware optimized portfolios, adds machine learning signals, and rigorously backtests a cost-aware trading strategy under purged cross-validation.
objectives: Derive mean-variance portfolio theory and the efficient frontier from first principles
Formulate portfolio construction as convex optimization with realistic constraints
Explain Brownian motion, Ito calculus, and the stochastic processes underlying asset prices
Build and validate risk models including value at risk and expected shortfall
Engineer financial features and labels while avoiding leakage and lookahead bias
Apply machine learning models to return prediction and signal generation
Design and backtest algorithmic trading strategies with transaction costs
Apply purged cross-validation and combinatorial backtesting to control overfitting
Optimize execution and rebalancing under uncertainty and turnover limits
Deliver a reproducible research pipeline from data to evaluated strategy
prerequisites: Python programming with pandas and NumPy

weeks.title: Markets, data, and returns
weeks.lecture: Asset classes, price and return definitions, log returns, stylized facts of financial time series, and stationarity. Data quality and survivorship bias.
weeks.practice: Set up the environment and load, clean, and resample market data into return series with pandas.
weeks.project: Initialize the project repository and assemble a clean multi-asset return dataset.

links.title: Introduction, Financial Terms and Concepts (MIT 18.S096)

weeks.title: Stochastic processes and Brownian motion
weeks.lecture: Random walks, Brownian motion, martingales, and the construction of continuous-time price processes. Properties of Wiener processes.
weeks.practice: Simulate Brownian motion and geometric Brownian motion price paths and study their statistics.
weeks.project: Add a Monte Carlo price simulator to the project toolkit.

links.title: Lecture 5: Stochastic Processes I (MIT 18.S096)
Stochastic Processes II (MIT 18.S096)

weeks.title: Ito calculus and pricing intuition
weeks.lecture: Ito's lemma, stochastic differential equations, geometric Brownian motion, and the Black-Scholes-Merton framework as risk-neutral pricing.
weeks.practice: Implement Ito-based simulation and a Black-Scholes pricer, comparing to Monte Carlo estimates.
weeks.project: Integrate a stochastic model used later for scenario generation.

links.title: Ito Calculus (MIT 18.S096)
Stochastic Differential Equations (MIT 18.S096)
Black-Scholes Formula, Risk-neutral Valuation (MIT 18.S096)

weeks.title: Mean-variance optimization
weeks.lecture: Expected return and covariance estimation, the mean-variance objective, the efficient frontier, and the tangency portfolio. Assumptions and limitations.
weeks.practice: Estimate the covariance matrix and compute the efficient frontier for the project universe.
weeks.project: Produce a baseline mean-variance portfolio for the project assets.

links.title: Portfolio Theory (MIT 18.S096)

weeks.title: Convex optimization for portfolios
weeks.lecture: Convex sets and functions, quadratic programs, duality, and constrained portfolio formulations with budget, long-only, and turnover constraints.
weeks.practice: Team presentation of the project specification: universe, objective, constraints, and evaluation plan.
weeks.project: Deliver the written and oral project specification milestone.

links.title: Stanford EE364A Convex Optimization I, Stephen Boyd, Lecture 1

weeks.title: Robust and advanced allocation
weeks.lecture: Estimation error, shrinkage, Black-Litterman views, risk parity, and hierarchical risk parity. Why naive optimization fails out of sample.
weeks.practice: Implement risk parity and a shrinkage-based optimizer with cvxpy and compare allocations.
weeks.project: Add robust allocation variants to the project portfolio engine.

links.title: Portfolio Management (MIT 18.S096)

weeks.title: Value at risk and expected shortfall
weeks.lecture: Risk measures, coherence, historical, parametric, and Monte Carlo value at risk, expected shortfall, and backtesting risk estimates.
weeks.practice: Compute and backtest value at risk and expected shortfall for the project portfolio.
weeks.project: Add a risk reporting module to the project.

links.title: Value At Risk (VaR) Models (MIT 18.S096)

weeks.title: Volatility, correlation, and stress
weeks.lecture: GARCH volatility, dynamic correlation, tail dependence, and stress and scenario testing for portfolio risk.
weeks.practice: Team interim presentation: portfolio engine, risk results, and open questions.
weeks.project: Deliver the interim presentation milestone with risk-aware portfolios.

links.title: Volatility Modeling (MIT 18.S096)

weeks.title: Features, labels, and leakage
weeks.lecture: Financial feature engineering, fractional differentiation, the triple-barrier labeling method, sample weighting, and avoiding lookahead bias.
weeks.practice: Engineer features and triple-barrier labels for the project assets.
weeks.project: Build the labeled supervised dataset for the project.

links.title: The 7 Reasons Most Machine Learning Funds Fail (Marcos Lopez de Prado, QuantCon 2018)

weeks.title: Models and purged cross-validation
weeks.lecture: Tree ensembles and regularized models for finance, feature importance, and purged and embargoed cross-validation to respect time structure.
weeks.practice: Train models with purged cross-validation and evaluate predictive signal quality.
weeks.project: Produce a validated predictive signal for the strategy.

links.title: Lecture 10: Regularized Pricing and Risk Models (MIT 18.S096)
The 7 Reasons Most Machine Learning Funds Fail (Marcos Lopez de Prado, QuantCon 2018)

weeks.title: Strategy design and execution
weeks.lecture: Signal to position mapping, bet sizing, transaction costs, slippage, and execution and rebalancing under turnover limits.
weeks.practice: Convert the predictive signal into a traded strategy with sizing and cost modeling.
weeks.project: Implement the trading strategy layer on top of the signal.

links.title: Portfolio Management (MIT 18.S096)

weeks.title: Backtesting and overfitting
weeks.lecture: Backtest design, walk-forward and combinatorial purged cross-validation, the deflated Sharpe ratio, and the dangers of selection bias.
weeks.practice: Run a rigorous backtest with cost and risk controls and compute performance statistics.
weeks.project: Finalize the backtest, metrics, and figures for the project.

links.title: The 7 Reasons Most Machine Learning Funds Fail (Marcos Lopez de Prado, QuantCon 2018)

weeks.title: Final strategy defense
weeks.lecture: Course synthesis: integrating optimization, risk, and machine learning into a defensible quantitative strategy and the limits of backtested evidence.
weeks.practice: Team final presentation with oral defense of methodology, results, and design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the final report, code, and backtest with oral defense.

links.title: The 7 Reasons Most Machine Learning Funds Fail (Marcos Lopez de Prado, QuantCon 2018)

project.summary: Teams build one end-to-end quantitative investment strategy for a chosen asset universe. Starting from clean data they construct optimized, risk-aware portfolios, add machine learning signals, and turn the result into a cost-aware algorithmic strategy that is rigorously backtested and defended.

tools: pandas: financial data wrangling and time series
NumPy: numerical computation and returns math
cvxpy: convex portfolio optimization models
PyPortfolioOpt: mean-variance, Black-Litterman, and risk models
scikit-learn: machine learning models and cross-validation
statsmodels: regression, time series, and volatility models
arch: GARCH volatility and risk estimation
vectorbt: vectorized backtesting of strategies
yfinance: market data acquisition for prototyping
Matplotlib: performance and risk visualization
SciPy: optimization and statistical routines
Jupyter: interactive notebooks for the running project

references.title: Advances in Financial Machine Learning
references.note: Labeling, purged cross-validation, and backtest pitfalls

references.title: Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives
references.note: Derivatives, risk-neutral pricing, and value at risk

references.title: Stochastic Calculus for Finance II: Continuous-Time Models
references.note: Brownian motion, Ito calculus, and risk-neutral pricing

references.title: Convex Optimization
references.note: Free online; foundation for portfolio optimization

QCF2.ai_usage: Students use AI coding assistants to vibe-code the quantitative research pipeline: generating pandas data-cleaning steps, drafting cvxpy and PyPortfolioOpt optimization models, and refactoring backtest loops from plain-language strategy specifications. They have the assistant call tools and MCP servers to pull and validate market data, generate synthetic price scenarios and unit tests that guard against lookahead bias and leakage, and scaffold scikit-learn pipelines with purged cross-validation. AI also helps evaluate and analyze results, computing and interpreting risk metrics, deflated Sharpe ratios, and backtest diagnostics, and challenging overfit strategies before they are defended.
